# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walk-through: 4-to-1 Multiplexer (MUX)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU-type)

> Design a 4-to-1 multiplexer using VHDL in **both** structural and behavioral styles. Behavioral input: `a, b, c, d` (data), `s1, s0` (select). Output: `y`.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Boolean Equation (Dataflow truth)

$$
y = \bar{s_1}\,\bar{s_0}\,a \;+\; \bar{s_1}\,s_0\,b \;+\; s_1\,\bar{s_0}\,c \;+\; s_1\,s_0\,d
$$

Each product term is an **AND**, then a 4-input **OR**. This naturally maps to 4 AND gates + 1 OR gate ⇒ **structural model**.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Behavioral VHDL (with detailed annotation)

```vhdl
library ieee;
use ieee.std_logic_1164.all;

entity mux4to1 is
  port ( a, b, c, d : in  std_logic;
         s           : in  std_logic_vector(1 downto 0);
         y           : out std_logic );
end mux4to1;

architecture behav of mux4to1 is
begin
  process(a, b, c, d, s)              -- sensitivity list: all inputs
  begin
    case s is                         -- one-hot decoding, equivalent to 4 ANDs
      when "00" => y <= a;            -- delta-delayed assignment to y
      when "01" => y <= b;
      when "10" => y <= c;
      when "11" => y <= d;
      when others => y <= 'X';        -- safety (metavalue)
    end case;
  end process;
end behav;
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. **Library inclusion** → brings in `std_logic` type.
2. **Entity** → external pin-out declaration.
3. **Process sensitivity list** = `(a,b,c,d,s)` ⇒ process re-evaluates on *any* input change.
4. **`case` statement** → synthesizes into a priority-encoded multiplexer.
5. **`when others` clause** → protects against un-encoded cases (good practice for KTU).

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Structural VHDL (building it from gates)

Assume a pre-defined 2-input AND gate `and2` and a 4-input OR gate `or4`:

```vhdl
architecture struct of mux4to1 is
  component and2
    port (i1, i2 : in  std_logic;
          o      : out std_logic);
  end component;

  component or4
    port (i1, i2, i3, i4 : in  std_logic;
          o              : out std_logic);
  end component;

  signal ns1, ns0, t0, t1, t2, t3 : std_logic;  -- internal nets
begin
  -- inverters
  ns1 <= not s(1);
  ns0 <= not s(0);

  -- 4 AND gates (named port mapping for clarity)
  u0 : and2 port map (i1 => ns1, i2 => ns0, o => t0);
  u1 : and2 port map (i1 => ns0, i2 => s(1), o => t1);
  u2 : and2 port map (i1 => s(0), i2 => ns1, o => t2);
  u3 : and2 port map (i1 => s(1), i2 => s(0), o => t3);

  -- 4-input OR
  u4 : or4 port map (i1 => t0, i2 => t1, i3 => t2, i4 => t3, o => y);
end struct;
```

### 3.1.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Aspect | Behavioral | Structural |
|---|---|---|
| **Question answered** | "What does it do?" | "How is it wired?" |
| **Synthesis** | High-level intent | Direct netlist |
| **Code size** | Compact | Verbose |
| **Readability** | Algorithm-like | Schematic-like |
| **Reusability** | Generic, parameterizable | Lower |
| **Testbench use** | Yes (golden model) | No (rarely) |
| **Simulation speed** | Slower (processes) | Faster (direct wiring) |

## 3.2 Derivation: 4-bit Ripple-Carry Adder using `generate`

### 3.2.1 Mathematical Foundation

For an N-bit adder, the carry chain is recursive:

$$
c_{i+1} = (a_i \cdot b_i) \;+\; (a_i \oplus b_i)\cdot c_i
$$

This is the **Full Adder (FA)** equation: `carry = (A AND B) OR ((A XOR B) AND Cin)`.

### 3.2.2 Full Adder Component

```vhdl
entity full_adder is
  port ( a, b, cin : in  std_logic;
         sum, cout : out std_logic );
end full_adder;

architecture fa_behav of full_adder is
begin
  process(a, b, cin)
  begin
    sum  <= a xor b xor cin;
    cout <= (a and b) or ((a xor b) and cin);
  end process;
end fa_behav;
```

### 3.2.3 N-bit Adder — Structural with `generate`

```vhdl
entity n_bit_adder is
  generic ( N : integer := 4 );
  port ( a, b : in  std_logic_vector(N-1 downto 0);
         cin   : in  std_logic;
         sum   : out std_logic_vector(N-1 downto 0);
         cout  : out std_logic );
end n_bit_adder;

architecture struct of n_bit_adder is
  component full_adder port (a, b, cin : in std_logic;
                             sum, cout : out std_logic);
  end component;
  signal c : std_logic_vector(N downto 0);
begin
  c(0) <= cin;
  gen_adder : for i in 0 to N-1 generate
    fa : full_adder port map ( a => a(i), b => b(i),
                                cin => c(i),  sum => sum(i),
                                cout => c(i+1) );
  end generate;
  cout <= c(N);
end struct;
```

**Logic walk-through (algebraic):**

1. Internal signal `c(0..N)` carries inter-stage carries. Size = **N+1** bits.
2. `c(0) <= cin;`  → initial carry.
3. `for i in 0 to N-1 generate`  → creates N FA instances automatically.
4. `cout <= c(N);`  → final carry-out after the last full adder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `std_logic_vector(N downto 0)` for `c`?** The `downto` is irrelevant for a *signal array*, but `0 to N` reads more naturally for carries (0→1→2→…→N). KTU allows both.

## 3.3 Derivation: D Flip-Flop Behavioral Model with Asynchronous Reset

$$
Q_{next} =
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } rst = 1 \\
D, & \text{if } clk \text{ has a rising edge} \\
Q, & \text{otherwise (hold)}
\end{cases}
$$

```vhdl
library ieee;
use ieee.std_logic_1164.all;

entity dff is
  port ( clk, rst, d : in  std_logic;
         q, qbar      : out std_logic );
end dff;

architecture behav of dff is
begin
  process(clk, rst)            -- asynchronous: BOTH clk and rst in list
  begin
    if rst = '1' then
      q    <= '0';
      qbar <= '1';
    elsif rising_edge(clk) then
      q    <= d;
      qbar <= not d;
    end if;
  end process;
end behav;
```

**Step-by-step transitions:**

- The `if rst = '1'` is checked **first** (priority), making reset asynchronous.
- The `elsif rising_edge(clk)` is the **only** synthesizable edge construct in KTU's accepted VHDL subset (alternatives: `clk'event and clk='1'` or `wait until clk = '1';`).
- All assignments are **signal assignments (`<=`)** so they take effect at the *end of process* ⇒ the inferred hardware is a flip-flop.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU question: *"What happens if you omit `rst` from the sensitivity list?"* → During simulation, the process would NOT re-evaluate when rst changes → **race condition / simulation mismatch**. Always list asynchronous signals.

## 3.4 Hybrid Modeling Example: ALU (Mixed Style)

Modern designs almost always **mix** styles. The ALU pattern is canonical:

```vhdl
architecture mixed of alu is
begin
  -- Dataflow for the result selector (concise boolean)
  with op select y <=
    a + b       when "000",
    a - b       when "001",
    a and b     when "010",
    a or b      when "011",
    a xor b     when "100",
    a sla 1     when "101",
    a sra 1     when "110",
    a           when others;

  -- Behavioral for the flags (sequential, FSM-like)
  process(a, b, op)
  begin
    case op is
      when "000" => zero <= '1' when (a+b) = 0 else '0';
      when "001" => zero <= '1' when (a-b) = 0 else '0';
      when others => zero <= '0';
    end case;
  end process;
end mixed;
```

This illustrates the **practical rule** in KTU: *Use the style that yields the clearest, most synthesizable code for each sub-block.*

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Q: Differentiate between structural and behavioral modeling styles in VHDL. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Structural Modeling | Behavioral Modeling |
|---|---|---|
| Description style | Interconnection of components | Algorithmic/functional description |
| Construct used | Component instantiation + port map | Process + sequential statements |
| Synthesis output | Gate-level netlist | High-level RTL, may infer memory |
| Example | Ripple-carry adder built from 1-bit FA | `if rising_edge(clk) then q <= d;` |

**Key phrase for full marks**: *"Structural modeling describes the circuit as a network of interconnected components (lower-level building blocks), while behavioral modeling describes what the circuit does using process statements with sequential constructs such as if, case, and loops, without specifying internal structure."*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q: Explain the use of the `generate` statement in VHDL with an example. Why is it classified as a concurrent statement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `generate` statement is a **concurrent statement** that allows multiple instances of a component to be created during elaboration, based on a `for` loop (or a static `if` condition). It is concurrent because, like all other concurrent statements, it is evaluated during elaboration — not during simulation — and is used purely to replicate hardware structure.

**Example (2-to-4 decoder using generate):**

```vhdl
gen: for i in 0 to 3 generate
  y(i) <= '1' when (i = sel) else '0';
end generate;
```

Here four concurrent assignments are created — one per bit of `sel`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Write a VHDL structural description of a **full adder** using two 2-input XOR gates, two AND gates, and one OR gate. Provide both the component declarations and the top-level architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a VHDL **behavioral description** of a 4-bit synchronous up-counter with an asynchronous active-low reset. The counter should count from 0 to 15 and wrap around. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Structural Full Adder

**Boolean equations (recap):**

$$
S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}
$$

$$
C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B))
$$

**Component declarations (2-input gates):**

```vhdl
library ieee;
use ieee.std_logic_1164.all;

entity fa_struct is
  port ( a, b, cin : in  std_logic;
         sum, cout : out std_logic );
end fa_struct;

architecture struct of fa_struct is
  component xor2 port (i1, i2 : in  std_logic; o : out std_logic); end component;
  component and2 port (i1, i2 : in  std_logic; o : out std_logic); end component;
  component or2  port (i1, i2 : in  std_logic; o : out std_logic); end component;

  signal w1, w2, w3 : std_logic;   -- internal nets
begin
  -- XOR chain
  u1 : xor2 port map (i1 => a,   i2 => b,   o => w1);
  u2 : xor2 port map (i1 => w1,  i2 => cin, o => sum);

  -- Carry logic
  u3 : and2 port map (i1 => a,   i2 => b,   o => w2);
  u4 : and2 port map (i1 => cin, i2 => w1,  o => w3);
  u5 : or2  port map (i1 => w2,  i2 => w3,  o => cout);
end struct;
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct entity: 1 mark
- Component declarations: 2 marks
- Signal declaration: 1 mark
- Port-map connections (correct netlist): 3 marks

#### Model Solution — (b) Behavioral 4-bit Synchronous Counter

```vhdl
library ieee;
use ieee.std_logic_1164.all;
use ieee.numeric_std.all;

entity up_counter is
  port ( clk      : in  std_logic;
         rst_n    : in  std_logic;                 -- active-low async reset
         count    : out std_logic_vector(3 downto 0) );
end up_counter;

architecture behav of up_counter is
  signal q : unsigned(3 downto 0) := (others => '0');
begin
  process(clk, rst_n)            -- async reset => both in sensitivity list
  begin
    if rst_n = '0' then
      q <= (others => '0');
    elsif rising_edge(clk) then
      q <= q + 1;                -- wraps naturally on 4 bits
    end if;
  end process;

  count <= std_logic_vector(q);
end behav;
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct entity with active-low reset: 1 mark
- Sensitivity list (clk, rst_n) and async reset logic: 2 marks
- Clocked process with `rising_edge`: 2 marks
- Correct wrap-around logic and output assignment: 2 marks

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat VHDL code, implement a **4-to-1 multiplexer** in **structural style** using only 2-input AND, OR, and NOT gates. Show the internal netlist. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement the **same 4-to-1 MUX** in **behavioral style** using a process and a `case` statement. Compare the synthesised hardware in terms of LUTs used (qualitative analysis). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Structural MUX

*(Refer to Section 3.1.4 above for the complete code.)* The structural implementation requires:

- 2 NOT gates for `s1, s0`
- 4 AND gates for the four minterms
- 1 OR gate (4-input)

**Expected Block Diagram (refer Section 4.4).**

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Boolean expansion (minterms): 1 mark
- Component declarations: 2 marks
- Internal signal declarations: 1 mark
- Correct instantiation and named port map: 3 marks

#### Model Solution — (b) Behavioral MUX

*(Refer to Section 3.1.3 above for the complete code.)* Highlights:

- A single process with `case` statement.
- Each `when` clause corresponds to one AND of the dataflow form.
- Synthesized result: a 4:1 LUT (or two cascaded 2:1 LUTs) on an FPGA — same number of logic resources as the structural version, but cleaner source.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct entity: 1 mark
- Sensitivity list (all 5 signals): 1 mark
- `case` statement with all four `when` branches: 3 marks
- `when others` clause and quality of explanation: 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing sensitivity list elements** → simulation mismatch. *Lose 1 mark per missing input.*
> 2. **Using `:=` instead of `<=` for a signal** → compile error. *Lose 2 marks.*
> 3. **Including `clk` but not `rst` in the sensitivity list of an async-reset flip-flop** → incorrect behaviour. *Lose 1 mark and risk zero on synthesis sub-part.*
> 4. **Positional port map with wrong order** → silent wrong circuit, no compile error. *Lose 2–3 marks.* Always prefer **named port map** in exams.
> 5. **Forgetting `end component;` in component declaration** → compilation error. *Lose 1 mark.*
> 6. **Using `wait for 10 ns;` in a synthesizable model** → testbench-only construct. *Lose 2 marks.*
> 7. **Confusing `for ... loop` inside a process (sequential) vs `for ... generate` outside (concurrent)** → KTU specifically tests this. *Lose 2 marks if the wrong one is used.*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-night KTU prep):**

- ✅ **Entity** = black-box interface. **Architecture** = inside description.
- ✅ **Structural** = `component` + `port map`. **Behavioral** = `process` + sequential statements.
- ✅ **Component declaration** comes **inside** the architecture, **before** `begin`.
- ✅ **Named port map** is safer than positional — use it in KTU answers.
- ✅ **`generate`** is **concurrent** (elaboration-time); **`for ... loop`** inside a process is **sequential** (simulation-time).
- ✅ **Signals (`<=`)** update at end of process; **variables (`:=`)** update immediately.
- ✅ **Clocked process** MUST use `rising_edge(clk)` (or `clk'event and clk='1'`).
- ✅ **Asynchronous reset** ⇒ both `clk` and `rst` in sensitivity list, with `if rst` checked **first**.
- ✅ **Sensitivity list** must include every signal read inside the process (VHDL-2008 relaxes this, but KTU assumes VHDL-1993).
- ✅ **`case`** must have all branches OR include `when others` to avoid inferred latches.
- ✅ **Mixed modeling** is the industry norm — not a sign of weakness.
- ✅ Always end the code with the **architecture label** matching its declaration: `end <label>;`.
- ✅ Standard library triplet for any KTU VHDL code: `ieee.std_logic_1164`, plus `ieee.numeric_std` for arithmetic on `unsigned/signed`.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Behavioral and Structural Modeling**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT415 - DIGITAL SYSTEMS AND VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Synthesis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synthesis** in VLSI Design is the automated process of transforming a high-level hardware description language (HDL) representation of a digital circuit—typically written at the **Register Transfer Level (RTL)** in Verilog or VHDL—into an optimized, technology-specific **gate-level netlist** (a structural netlist of standard cells from a target Standard Cell Library). This gate-level netlist is functionally equivalent to the original RTL but is composed of physical logic gates (AND, OR, NAND, NOR, Flip-Flops, multiplexers, etc.) drawn from a specific **Process Design Kit (PDK)** / Standard Cell Library of a target fabrication technology (e.g., 45nm, 28nm CMOS). The synthesis tool is also called a **Logic Synthesizer**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Synthesis is the pivotal bridge between the *Front-End Design* (HDL/RTL coding and simulation) and the *Back-End Design* (Floorplanning, Placement, and Routing). The output of synthesis is a structural netlist in the form of `.v` (Verilog gate-level) or `.sdf` (Standard Delay Format) accompanied by synthesis reports. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are an architect who has drafted a building in a generic blueprint language (the **RTL**), describing rooms, doors, and functional flow without specifying the exact bricks. The **Logic Synthesizer** is like a master builder who: 1. **Translates** the blueprint into a specific style (e.g., Kerala traditional vs. Modern Concrete). 2. **Optimizes** the design to use the fewest, most efficient materials. 3. **Maps** every component to actual physical resources (specific bricks, doors, and windows available in the supplier's catalog—the **Standard Cell Library**). The final output is a **construction-ready plan** (the **gate-level netlist**) that maintains all functional requirements but is concrete, cost-effective, and manufacturable in the chosen technology node. ### Key Terminology & Metrics - **RTL (Register Transfer Level):** Behavioral/structural description at the data-flow level between registers. - **Netlist:** A textual/graphical description of circuit connectivity (gates and their interconnections). - **Standard Cell Library:** Pre-characterized logic cells with defined area, timing, and power. - **Gate-Level Netlist:** The synthesized output, using only standard cells. - **Technology Mapping:** The process of binding generic boolean logic to a specific set of standard cells. - **Fanout:** The number of loads a gate drives. - **Critical Path:** The longest timing path in the design that determines maximum clock frequency. - **Slack:** Difference between required arrival time and actual arrival time of a signal. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Definition (must memorize):** Synthesis = Translation + Logic Optimization + Technology Mapping. This exact three-stage decomposition is the most frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU ESE. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Logic Synthesis The Logic Synthesizer executes a sequential pipeline of algorithms. The KTU Board Examiner expects a student to list and explain these steps clearly. #### **Step 1: Translation (Elaboration)** - The HDL (Verilog/VHDL) RTL code is parsed and converted into an intermediate format. - Common internal representation: **Generic Boolean Network** (a directed acyclic graph, DAG, of logic gates with no technology-specific constraints). - The synthesizer ignores `initial` blocks and timing controls (`#delay`) during synthesis; only synthesizable constructs survive. - Output: A technology-independent netlist. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often think `initial` blocks and `#delay` statements are synthesizable. They are NOT. They are simulation-only constructs. Mentioning this explicitly in the answer script earns an extra **½ mark** with KTU examiners. #### **Step 2: Logic Optimization / Boolean Minimization** - The generic boolean network is reduced using algorithms such as: - **Two-Level Logic Minimization:** Quine-McCluskey algorithm, Espresso algorithm. - **Multi-Level Logic Minimization:** Algebraic factorization, kernel extraction, common subexpression elimination. - Goal: Minimize area, power, and delay without altering functionality. - Techniques: - Constant propagation - Dead code elimination - Resource sharing (e.g., one adder reused for multiple operations in different states) #### **Step 3: Technology Mapping (Binding)** - The optimized generic network is mapped onto the target **Standard Cell Library**. - **Subject Graph Matching:** The generic DAG is matched against library cells (NAND2, NOR3, AOI21, OAI22, etc.). - **Cost Functions** considered: - **Area Cost:** Total cell area in $\mu m^2$. - **Delay Cost:** Critical path delay. - **Power Cost:** Dynamic + leakage power. - Output: A **technology-mapped netlist** containing only library cells with `.lib` references. ### 2.2 Constraints That Drive Synthesis The synthesizer requires **Synthesis Constraints** to optimize meaningfully. These are typically specified in **Synopsys Design Constraints (SDC)** format. | Constraint Type | Purpose | Example SDC Command | |---|---|---| | **Clock** | Defines clock period & waveform | `create_clock -name CLK -period 10 [get_ports clk]` | | **Input Delay** | External arrival time at inputs | `set_input_delay -max 2.0 -clock CLK [get_ports IN]` | | **Output Delay** | Required time at outputs | `set_output_delay -max 2.0 -clock CLK [get_ports OUT]` | | **False Path** | Paths to ignore in timing | `set_false_path -from [get_clocks CLK1] -to [get_clocks CLK2]` | | **Multicycle Path** | Paths that take >1 cycle | `set_multicycle_path 2 -setup -from A -to B` | | **Area Constraint** | Limits maximum area | `set_max_area 5000` | | **Operating Conditions** | PVT (Process, Voltage, Temp) | `set_operating_conditions -max WORST` | ### 2.3 Types of Synthesis Reports A good KTU answer should mention what reports a synthesizer generates: - **Timing Report:** Lists worst negative slack (WNS), total negative slack (TNS), critical path. - **Area Report:** Total cell area, combinational vs. sequential breakdown. - **Power Report:** Dynamic, static, and total power consumption. - **QoR (Quality of Results) Report:** Summary of all metrics. > [!NOTE] > **Key Industrial Term:** The entire synthesis process is referred to as **RTL-to-GDSII** at the front-end level, and the synthesis stage specifically produces the input to **Place-and-Route (P&R)** tools like Cadence Innovus or Synopsys ICC. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | |---|---|---| | **Clock Period** | $T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency in **Hz** | | **Slack Equation** | $S = T_{req} - T_{arr}$ | $T_{req}$: required time; $T_{arr}$: actual arrival time | | **Setup Slack** | $S_{setup} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} + T_{unc})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ for setup-met | | **Hold Slack** | $S_{hold} = (T_{cq} + T_{comb}) - T_{hold} - T_{unc}$ | Must be $\geq 0$ for hold-met | | **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$: switching activity; $C_L$: load capacitance | | **Static Power** | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $I_{leak}$: leakage current | | **Maximum Delay** | $T_{pd,max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{d,i}$ | Sum of per-stage propagation delays along critical path | | **Fanout Limit** | $FO_{max} = \frac{I_{drive}}{I_{input}}$ | Maximum gates a driver can reliably drive | | **Path Delay Constraint** | $T_{comb,max} \leq T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{setup}$ | Combinational logic budget per cycle | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE (v10):** In KTU board answer sheets, write the slack equation $S = T_{req} - T_{arr}$ explicitly before plugging in numbers. Examiners award partial marks for the *setup* of the formula, even if the final calculation goes wrong. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ASIC Design:** Synthesis is the mandatory step before sending the design to a foundry (TSMC, Intel, Samsung) for fabrication. - **FPGA Design:** Although the *synthesis* term is used, FPGAs use **technology mapping onto LUTs (Look-Up Tables)** by tools like Xilinx Vivado or Intel Quartus. - **Design Exploration:** Synthesis allows rapid **Design Space Exploration (DSE)**—trying different architectures and measuring their Area, Timing, and Power (ATP) trade-offs. - **Verification Gate:** The synthesized netlist undergoes **Gate-Level Simulation (GLS)** with SDF back-annotation to verify timing correctness. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Setup Time Slack Equation Consider a sequential circuit with a flip-flop $FF1$ driving combinational logic, which in turn drives another flip-flop $FF2$. Both flip-flops are clocked by the same clock $CLK$ with period $T_{clk}$. **Step 1: Identify the data launch event.** At the active edge of clock cycle $n$, the output of $FF1$ becomes valid after the **Clock-to-Q delay**, $T_{cq}$. $$ T_{arr,FF1-out} = T_{launch} + T_{cq} $$ This represents the actual time when data leaves $FF1$. **Step 2: Identify the combinational propagation.** The signal then traverses the combinational logic with a total propagation delay $T_{comb}$. $$ T_{arr,FF2-in} = T_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} $$ **Step 3: Identify the data capture event.** The data must arrive at $FF2$ at least one **setup time**, $T_{setup}$, before the next active clock edge at $T_{launch} + T_{clk}$. **Step 4: Define the required arrival time.** $$ T_{req,FF2} = T_{launch} + T_{clk} - T_{setup} $$ **Step 5: Subtract to get the slack.** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{setup} &= T_{req,FF2} - T_{arr,FF2-in} \\ &= \left( T_{launch} + T_{clk} - T_{setup} \right) - \left( T_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} \right) \\ &= T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{setup} \end{aligned} $$ For the design to meet timing: $S_{setup} \geq 0$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Hold Time Slack Equation The hold check ensures that data launched at clock edge $n$ does not propagate too quickly and corrupt the data being captured at the *same* clock edge $n$ in $FF2$. **Step 1: Required time for hold is the same edge.** $$ T_{req,hold} = T_{launch} + T_{hold} $$ **Step 2: Actual arrival time at FF2 input.** $$ T_{arr,FF2-in} = T_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} $$ **Step 3: Compute hold slack.** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{hold} &= T_{arr,FF2-in} - T_{req,hold} \\ &= \left( T_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} \right) - \left( T_{launch} + T_{hold} \right) \\ &= T_{cq} + T_{comb} - T_{hold} \end{aligned} $$ For hold-met: $S_{hold} \geq 0$. ### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Operating Frequency From the setup slack expression, the minimum allowable clock period is found by setting $S_{setup} = 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= T_{clk,min} - T_{cq} - T_{comb,max} - T_{setup} \\ T_{clk,min} &= T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} + T_{setup} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the maximum clock frequency is: $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} + T_{setup}} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A digital block has $T_{cq} = 0.3$ ns, $T_{comb,max} = 4.2$ ns, $T_{setup} = 0.2$ ns, $T_{hold} = 0.1$ ns, and the clock period is $T_{clk} = 5$ ns. Determine: (a) Setup slack, (b) Hold slack, (c) Maximum operating frequency. **Solution:** **(a) Setup Slack:** $$ S_{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb,max} - T_{setup} = 5 - 0.3 - 4.2 - 0.2 = 0.3 \text{ ns} $$ Since $S_{setup} > 0$, **setup is met**. **(b) Hold Slack:** $$ S_{hold} = T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} - T_{hold} = 0.3 + 4.2 - 0.1 = 4.4 \text{ ns} $$ Since $S_{hold} > 0$, **hold is met**. **(c) Maximum Operating Frequency:** $$ T_{clk,min} = 0.3 + 4.2 + 0.2 = 4.7 \text{ ns} $$ $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{4.7 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 212.77 \text{ MHz} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Strategy:** Always write the *units* explicitly (ns, MHz). Examiners often deduct ½ mark for missing units in numerical problems. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Synthesis The diagram below maps the complete synthesis flow, including the inputs, intermediate stages, and outputs of a typical logic synthesis tool (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[RTL HDL Source Code
Verilog or VHDL] --> B[HDL Parsing
and Elaboration] B --> C{Translation Stage
Generic Boolean Network DAG} C --> D[Logic Optimization Stage
Two-Level and Multi-Level Minimization] D --> E[Technology Independent
Optimized Netlist] E --> F[Technology Mapping Stage
Subject Graph Matching] G[Standard Cell Library
Liberty .lib File] --> F H[Synthesis Constraints SDC
Clock, Area, Power, PVT] --> F F --> I[Technology Mapped
Gate Level Netlist] I --> J[Post Synthesis Reports] J --> K1[Timing Report
WNS, TNS, Critical Path] J --> K2[Area Report
Total Cell Area in um2] J --> K3[Power Report
Dynamic and Static] I --> L[Gate Level Simulation GLS
with SDF Back Annotation] L --> M[Verified Gate Level Netlist
Ready for Place and Route] ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Three-Stage Decomposition View ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Phase1[Phase 1: Translation] A1[RTL Code] --> A2[Parse HDL] A2 --> A3[Build GTECH Netlist
Generic Technology] end subgraph Phase2[Phase 2: Optimization] A3 --> B1[Constant Propagation] B1 --> B2[Dead Code Elimination] B2 --> B3[Resource Sharing] B3 --> B4[Boolean Minimization
Espresso Algorithm] end subgraph Phase3[Phase 3: Technology Mapping] B4 --> C1[Load Standard Cell Library] C1 --> C2[Match DAG to Library Cells] C2 --> C3[Optimize for Area, Delay, Power] C3 --> C4[Mapped Netlist .v File] end ``` ### 4.3 Mapping of Inputs to Synthesis Output (Architecture Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart TB IN1[Verilog RTL] --> SYN[Logic Synthesizer] IN2[VHDL RTL] --> SYN IN3[SDC Constraints File] --> SYN IN4[Standard Cell Library .lib] --> SYN IN5[PVT Operating Conditions] --> SYN SYN --> OUT1[Gate Level Netlist .v] SYN --> OUT2[SDF Delay File .sdf] SYN --> OUT3[SAIF Switching Activity] SYN --> OUT4[Synthesis Reports .rpt] OUT1 --> PNR[Place and Route Tool] OUT2 --> PNR PNR --> GDSII[GDSII Layout for Fabrication] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always draw a *labeled block diagram* of the synthesis flow. Examiners allocate **2 to 3 marks** specifically for the correctness and clarity of the diagram. Use rectangular blocks with arrows clearly showing data flow. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term **Synthesis** as used in the VLSI design flow. List the three main stages of logic synthesis. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Synthesis** is the process of converting an HDL-based Register Transfer Level (RTL) description of a digital design into an optimized gate-level netlist using a specific Standard Cell Library. > > The three main stages of logic synthesis are: > > 1. **Translation (Elaboration):** Converts the HDL code into a technology-independent boolean network. > 2. **Logic Optimization:** Applies boolean minimization and algebraic transformations to reduce area and improve timing. > 3. **Technology Mapping:** Maps the optimized logic onto specific standard cells from the target library, considering area, delay, and power constraints. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between **RTL Simulation** and **Gate-Level Simulation (GLS)**. Why is GLS required after synthesis? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | RTL Simulation | Gate-Level Simulation (GLS) | |---|---|---| | **Abstraction** | High-level (behavioral/RTL) | Low-level (gate/transistor) | | **Timing** | No real timing, `#delay` only | Real delays from SDF back-annotation | | **Purpose** | Functional verification of design intent | Verifies timing and post-synthesis correctness | | **Speed** | Fast | Slower (due to timing calculations) | | **Library** | Not required | Requires standard cell library | > GLS is required after synthesis to ensure that the synthesized netlist is **functionally equivalent** to the original RTL and that **timing constraints** (setup and hold) are met in the technology-mapped implementation. --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete **VLSI synthesis flow** starting from HDL input to gate-level netlist output. List and explain the role of each input file. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concepts of **False Path** and **Multicycle Path** in synthesis constraints. Provide an SDC example for each. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) VLSI Synthesis Flow (7 marks):** The synthesis flow consists of the following stages, mapped against the required input files: 1. **HDL Parsing:** The Verilog/VHDL file is parsed and checked for syntax/semantic errors. Constructs like `initial` and `#delay` are stripped. 2. **Translation:** The synthesizable HDL is elaborated into a **generic boolean network (GTECH netlist)**—an internal graph representation independent of any technology. 3. **Logic Optimization:** The boolean network is optimized using algorithms like **Quine-McCluskey** (two-level) and **Algebraic Factorization** (multi-level) to reduce gate count, area, and power. 4. **Technology Mapping:** The optimized network is bound to the **Standard Cell Library (.lib file)**, mapping each boolean function to a specific cell (NAND2, AOI21, FF, etc.). 5. **Optimization Iterations:** The synthesizer iterates between mapping and optimization to meet all SDC constraints. 6. **Report Generation:** Final reports for timing, area, and power are generated. **Required Input Files (role of each):** - **HDL Source (.v / .vhd):** Provides the functional RTL specification. - **Standard Cell Library (.lib):** Contains timing, area, and power characteristics of every cell in the technology. - **SDC Constraints (.sdc):** Specifies clock period, input/output delays, false paths, multicycle paths, and area limits. - **Operating Conditions File:** Defines PVT corners (Worst, Typical, Best). **(b) False Path and Multicycle Path (7 marks):** **False Path:** A timing path that is **logically impossible to be exercised** or does not need to be optimized for timing. The synthesizer skips it during timing analysis. Example: A reset signal path crossing clock domains. ```tcl set_false_path -from [get_pins U1/reset_reg/Q] -to [get_pins U2/data_reg/D] ``` **Multicycle Path:** A path that is allowed to take **more than one clock cycle** to propagate, usually because of intentional design choice. By default, a path is single-cycle. The path delay constraint is relaxed. ```tcl set_multicycle_path 2 -setup -from [get_pins U1/multi_reg/Q] -to [get_pins U2/cap_reg/D] ``` This tells the synthesizer that the signal from `multi_reg` to `cap_reg` has **2 clock cycles** to settle, effectively doubling the allowed combinational delay. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** What is a **Standard Cell Library**? Explain its role in synthesis. List any four timing-related attributes that a typical library cell provides. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the equations for **Setup Slack** and **Hold Slack** for a sequential path. A digital block has $T_{cq} = 0.4$ ns, $T_{comb,max} = 3.8$ ns, $T_{setup} = 0.3$ ns, $T_{hold} = 0.2$ ns, and $T_{clk} = 5$ ns. Compute the setup slack, hold slack, and the maximum operating frequency. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Standard Cell Library (7 marks):** A **Standard Cell Library** is a collection of pre-designed, pre-characterized logic gates and sequential elements (inverters, NAND, NOR, AOI, OAI, flip-flops, latches, etc.) that form the building blocks of any synthesized ASIC design. Each cell is characterized for: - **Area** in $\mu m^2$ - **Timing** (propagation delay, setup/hold times, clock-to-Q delay) - **Power** (dynamic and leakage) - **Functionality** (boolean function) - **Loading characteristics** (input pin capacitance, output drive strength) **Role in Synthesis:** The synthesizer consults the library during the technology mapping stage to bind each logic function to a physical cell. Without the library, the synthesizer cannot produce a technology-specific netlist. **Four Timing Attributes:** 1. **Intrinsic Delay:** Delay of the cell when no load is connected. 2. **Input Pin Capacitance:** Load presented at each input pin. 3. **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** Time for output to switch after input transition. 4. **Setup Time / Hold Time:** Required stability times for sequential cells. 5. **Clock-to-Q Delay ($T_{cq}$):** Delay from active clock edge to Q-output update. **(b) Slack Derivations and Numerical Computation (7 marks):** The **Setup Slack** derivation begins by considering the data launched from $FF1$ at clock edge $n$. After clock-to-Q delay $T_{cq}$ and combinational delay $T_{comb}$, the data must arrive at $FF2$ at least $T_{setup}$ before the next active clock edge. The required arrival time is: $$ T_{req} = T_{clk} - T_{setup} $$ The actual arrival time is: $$ T_{arr} = T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} $$ The setup slack is therefore: $$ S_{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb,max} - T_{setup} $$ The **Hold Slack** derivation considers that data launched at clock edge $n$ must not arrive too quickly at $FF2$ and corrupt the data being captured at the *same* edge. The hold slack is: $$ S_{hold} = T_{cq} + T_{comb,min} - T_{hold} $$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: $T_{cq} = 0.4$ ns, $T_{comb,max} = 3.8$ ns, $T_{setup} = 0.3$ ns, $T_{hold} = 0.2$ ns, $T_{clk} = 5$ ns. **Setup Slack:** $$ S_{setup} = 5 - 0.4 - 3.8 - 0.3 = 0.5 \text{ ns} $$ Since $S_{setup} > 0$, **setup is satisfied**. **Hold Slack:** $$ S_{hold} = 0.4 + 3.8 - 0.2 = 4.0 \text{ ns} $$ Since $S_{hold} > 0$, **hold is satisfied**. **Maximum Operating Frequency:** $$ T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} + T_{setup} = 0.4 + 3.8 + 0.3 = 4.5 \text{ ns} $$ $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{4.5 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 222.22 \text{ MHz} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse $T_{clk,min}$ with $T_{clk}$:** Many students plug the given $T_{clk}$ into $f_{max}$ calculation. The correct procedure is to find $T_{clk,min}$ from the slack equation *set to zero*, then invert. > 2. **Units Matter:** Always convert nanoseconds to seconds (multiply by $10^{-9}$) when computing frequency in Hz. Missing unit conversion is the #1 reason for losing a full mark in numerical problems. > 3. **SDC Syntax:** Examiners may deduct marks for syntactically incorrect SDC commands. Always use the official Synopsys SDC syntax: `set_false_path -from -to `. > 4. **Hold vs. Setup:** Do NOT swap the two slack formulas. The hold check is for the *same* clock edge; the setup check is for the *next* clock edge. A common error is writing $S_{hold} = T_{clk} - T_{hold} - T_{cq} - T_{comb}$, which is conceptually wrong. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (MUST MEMORIZE):** - **Synthesis** = Translation + Logic Optimization + Technology Mapping. - Output of synthesis is a **gate-level netlist** in technology-mapped standard cells. - **Standard Cell Library (.lib)** is mandatory for technology mapping; without it, only a generic netlist is produced. - **RTL constructs that are NOT synthesizable:** `initial` blocks, `#delay` statements, `forever`, `force`/`release`, `fork`/`join` in non-blocking style, `$display`/`$monitor`. - **SDC Constraints** include: `create_clock`, `set_input_delay`, `set_output_delay`, `set_false_path`, `set_multicycle_path`, `set_max_area`. - **Setup Slack** formula: $S_{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{setup}$ (must be $\geq 0$). - **Hold Slack** formula: $S_{hold} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} - T_{hold}$ (must be $\geq 0$). - **Maximum Frequency** formula: $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} + T_{setup}}$. - **Dynamic Power** formula: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ (this is the CMOS power equation frequently asked in Part A). - **Critical Path** = the longest combinational delay path; determines the maximum clock frequency. - **False Path** = ignored during timing analysis (e.g., reset paths, static configuration paths). - **Multicycle Path** = path allowed to take N clock cycles; constraint relaxed. - **GLS (Gate-Level Simulation)** with SDF back-annotation is required *after* synthesis to verify timing and functionality. - **Industrial Tools:** Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus, Xilinx Vivado (for FPGA). - **Two-Level Minimization:** Quine-McCluskey, Espresso. - **Multi-Level Minimization:** Algebraic factorization, kernel extraction, common subexpression elimination. - **Synthesizer Reports:** Timing (WNS, TNS), Area, Power, QoR. - The **Place and Route (P&R)** tool takes the gate-level netlist + SDF as input and produces the **GDSII layout** for fabrication. - The **PVT (Process-Voltage-Temperature)** corners (Worst, Typical, Best) are specified to the synthesizer for robust design.

topics.title: Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT415 - DIGITAL SYSTEMS AND VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VLSI Simulation** is the software-driven emulation of a digital or mixed-signal circuit's behavior using mathematical models, hardware description languages (HDLs), and event-driven algorithms to verify functional correctness, timing performance, and power characteristics **before** physical fabrication.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Simulation** is defined as the computational process of predicting the logical and temporal behavior of a designed circuit by applying input stimuli (test vectors) to its **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** model and observing the resulting output waveforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Categorization in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **Logic Simulation** — discrete-event, Boolean behavior.
> - **Circuit Simulation (SPICE-class)** — solves Kirchhoff's equations.
> - **Switch-Level Simulation** — models transistors as switches.
> - **Behavioral / RTL Simulation** — functional verification of HDL code.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of VLSI Simulation as a **virtual wind tunnel for a chip**. 

Before Boeing builds a real airplane, they put a digital twin inside a computer and force **simulated hurricane winds** (input test vectors) against it to see if the wings bend or break. Similarly, before TSMC fabricates a chip (which costs millions of dollars per mask set), VLSI engineers build a **digital twin** (the HDL model) and apply **simulated input signals** to it, then watch the outputs on a virtual oscilloscope.

**Why is it mandatory?**
1. **Fabrication is irreversible** — you cannot "un-print" a transistor.
2. **Visibility is zero** — once fabricated, internal nodes of a chip are physically inaccessible.
3. **Cost** — a single 7nm mask set costs **> $20 million USD**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that simulation is the **primary verification methodology** for digital design, with **at least 70% of the design cycle** dedicated to writing testbenches, simulating, and debugging.

### Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Testbench** | HDL code that generates stimulus and checks response |
| **Event** | A change in signal value at a specific time |
| **Time Wheel** | Data structure used by event-driven simulators |
| **Test Vector** | A specific input combination applied during simulation |
| **Self-Checking** | Testbench that automatically compares DUT output to expected |
| **Code Coverage** | Metric indicating % of HDL lines executed during simulation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The VLSI Design Verification Flow

The simulation process follows a strict hierarchical order matching abstraction levels:

1. **Behavioral / RTL Simulation** — fastest, least detailed.
2. **Gate-Level Simulation** — synthesized netlist, timing-aware.
3. **Switch-Level Simulation** — transistor-on/transistor-off models.
4. **Transistor / Circuit Simulation** — full SPICE accuracy, slowest.

### Types of Simulation — Deep Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes simulation into the following primary types:

#### A. Logic Simulation
Operates on Boolean values: **`0`, `1`, `X` (unknown), `Z` (high-impedance)**.
- **Compiled Simulation:** HDL is translated into an optimized C/assembly program; runs very fast.
- **Event-Driven (Interpreted) Simulation:** Simulator maintains a clock; only re-evaluates gates whose inputs *change*.

#### B. Circuit Simulation (SPICE)
Solves the full nonlinear differential equations of the circuit using **Newton-Raphson iteration** and **sparse matrix solvers**.

#### C. Switch-Level Simulation
Models MOS transistors as ideal switches with **resistive (on)** and **infinite (off)** states. Bridges logic and circuit simulation.

#### D. Mixed-Signal Simulation
Combines analog solvers (SPICE kernels) with digital event-driven engines.

### The Event-Driven Simulation Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most asked concept** in KTU University Exams for Module 4.

The event-driven simulator maintains the following core data structures:
1. **Event Queue (Time Wheel)** — sorted list of future events.
2. **Circuit Netlist** — topology of gates/transistors.
3. **Current Signal Values** — state of every net at the current time.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize all signals; push initial events into the time wheel.
2. Pop the next event from the queue.
3. Update the signal value at the scheduled time.
4. **Fan-out:** for every gate fed by this signal, re-evaluate the gate's output.
5. If the gate's output *changed*, schedule a new event in the queue.
6. Repeat until the queue is empty.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Gate evaluation delay | $t_{pd} \le t_{pd,\max}$ | Propagation delay bound for combinational logic |
| Setup time check | $t_{clk} \ge t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su}$ | Hold for sequential correctness |
| Hold time check | $t_{cq} + t_{cd} \ge t_{hold}$ | Latch transparency constraint |
| Event count metric | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i$ | Total transitions during simulation |
| Simulation speedup | $S = \frac{T_{behav}}{T_{gate}}$ | Ratio of abstraction level speeds |
| Logic level values | $\{0, 1, X, Z\}$ | Standard 4-state simulation logic |
| Coverage metric | $C = \frac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % of HDL lines hit by testbench |
| Testbench events | $N_{ev} = T_{sim} \cdot f_{clk} \cdot N_{sig}$ | Approximate number of events scheduled |

> [!WARNING]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not bare `\|`) inside markdown tables to avoid breaking table rendering.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-silicon verification** in companies like Intel, AMD, Qualcomm.
- **Functional verification** consumes 60–70% of the entire ASIC design cycle.
- **Regression testing** — re-running thousands of testbenches after every RTL change.
- **Power estimation** using switching activity gathered from simulation traces (`.vcd` files).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Setup Time Constraint (Sequential Logic Timing)

The KTU 2024 Module 4 frequently asks for the derivation of the **minimum clock period** for a sequential design. We will derive this exhaustively.

**Given:**
- A flip-flop with clock-to-Q delay: $t_{cq}$
- Combinational logic delay between two flip-flops: $t_{comb}$
- Setup time of the destination flip-flop: $t_{su}$
- Clock period: $T_{clk}$

**Step 1 — Identify the signal launch event.**
At the rising edge of clock cycle $n$, the source flip-flop launches a new data value onto its $Q$ output.

**Step 2 — Add the clock-to-Q delay.**
The data first appears on the destination's $D$ input after the clock-to-Q propagation delay:

$$t_{arrival,\text{launch}} = t_{cq}$$

**Step 3 — Propagate through combinational logic.**
The signal must traverse the entire combinational cloud between the two flip-flops:

$$t_{arrival,\text{at\_D}} = t_{cq} + t_{comb}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to the next clock edge.**
For the destination flip-flop to reliably capture the data, the data must be stable **at least $t_{su}$ before the next rising clock edge**:

$$t_{arrival,\text{at\_D}} + t_{su} \le T_{clk}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expression for $t_{arrival,\text{at\_D}}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su} &\le T_{clk} \\
T_{clk} &\ge t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su} \\
\therefore \quad f_{clk,\max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk,\min}} = \frac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** used during gate-level simulation timing analysis.

---

### Derivation 2: Hold Time Constraint

The hold time is checked **within the same clock cycle** (or between two consecutive edges) to ensure data is not overwritten too quickly.

**Step 1 — Minimum data arrival time.**
The fastest the data can reach the destination $D$ input is bounded by the **contamination delay** of the flip-flop and combinational logic:

$$t_{arrival,\min} = t_{cq,\min} + t_{cd}$$

where $t_{cd}$ is the **contamination delay** (shortest possible delay) of the combinational logic.

**Step 2 — Hold time requirement.**
The destination flip-flop requires the data to remain stable for at least $t_{hold}$ after the active clock edge:

$$t_{arrival,\min} \ge t_{hold}$$

**Step 3 — Final inequality:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{cq,\min} + t_{cd} &\ge t_{hold} \\
\therefore \quad t_{cd} &\ge t_{hold} - t_{cq,\min}
\end{aligned}$$

If this is violated, a **hold violation** is reported by the gate-level simulator.

---

### Derivation 3: Event-Drive Simulation Cost

For an $N$-gate circuit simulated over $T$ time units, the work performed is:

**Step 1 — Count of input events.**
Assume each gate has an average of $k$ input transitions per unit time.

**Step 2 — Number of gate evaluations.**

$$N_{eval} = N \cdot k \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Speedup from event-driven skipping.**
Without event-driven optimization, every gate is evaluated every delta cycle, giving $N \cdot T_{total}$. The speedup factor is:

$$S_{event} = \frac{N \cdot T_{total}}{N \cdot k \cdot T} = \frac{T_{total}}{k \cdot T}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
For sparse input activity ($k \ll 1$), the event-driven simulator is **orders of magnitude faster** than the oblivious re-evaluation method.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Simulation Types

| Attribute | Behavioral | Gate-Level | Switch-Level | Circuit (SPICE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Abstraction | Highest | Medium | Low-Medium | Lowest |
| Speed | **Fastest** | Fast | Slow | **Slowest** |
| Accuracy | Functional only | Logical + Timing | Approximate | **Exact** |
| Signal States | Logic (4-state) | Logic (4-state) | Logic | Continuous (analog) |
| Use Case | Algorithm validation | Post-synthesis verify | Transistor design | Analog/parasitic |
| KTU Marks Weight | High | **Very High** | Medium | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A: VLSI Simulation Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    specA[RTL Design] --> stepA[HDL Compilation]
    stepA --> stepB[Elaborate Design]
    stepB --> stepC[Generate Testbench]
    stepC --> stepD[Compile Simulator]
    stepD --> stepE{Run Simulation}
    stepE --> stepF[Waveform Viewer]
    stepE --> stepG[Log File Output]
    stepF --> stepH{Pass or Fail}
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH -->|Fail| stepI[Debug RTL]
    stepI --> specA
    stepH -->|Pass| stepJ[Gate Level Netlist]
    stepJ --> stepK[Gate Level Simulation]
    stepK --> stepL[Timing Analysis]
    stepL --> stepM[Sign Off]
```

### Diagram B: Event-Driven Simulation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    initA[Initialize Netlist] --> initB[Push Initial Events]
    initB --> loopA{Pop Next Event}
    loopA --> loopB[Update Signal Value]
    loopB --> loopC[Find Fanout Gates]
    loopC --> loopD{Re Evaluate Gate}
    loopD -->|Output Changed| loopE[Schedule New Event]
    loopD -->|No Change| loopA
    loopE --> loopA
    loopA -->|Queue Empty| endA[End Simulation]
```

### Diagram C: Abstraction Hierarchy in VLSI Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lvlA[System Level] --> lvlB[Behavioral Level]
    lvlB --> lvlC[RTL Level]
    lvlC --> lvlD[Gate Level]
    lvlD --> lvlE[Switch Level]
    lvlE --> lvlF[Transistor Circuit Level]
    lvlF --> lvlG[Layout Level]
```

> [!NOTE]
> As we descend this hierarchy, **simulation accuracy increases** but **simulation speed decreases exponentially**. KTU questions often ask students to identify the correct level for a given verification goal.

### Diagram D: Testbench Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tbA[Stimulus Generator] --> dutA[Design Under Test]
    dutA --> monA[Output Monitor]
    monA --> chkA[Reference Checker]
    chkA --> rptA[Pass Fail Reporter]
    chkA -->|Mismatch| dbgA[Debug Waveform]
```

### Diagram E: Simulation Event Time Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    twA[Time 0 Bucket] --> twB[Time 1 Bucket]
    twB --> twC[Time 2 Bucket]
    twC --> twD[Time T Bucket]
    twA --> evtA[Event A0]
    twA --> evtB[Event A1]
    twB --> evtC[Event B0]
    twD --> evtD[Event T0]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define event-driven simulation. List the data structures used by an event-driven simulator.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Event-driven simulation is a discrete-event simulation technique in which a logic/circuit simulator only re-evaluates a gate or component when one of its input signals *changes* (i.e., generates an "event").
- **Data structures (2 marks):**
  1. **Time Wheel / Event Queue** — stores pending events ordered by simulation time.
  2. **Netlist / Circuit Model** — describes topology and gate functionality.
  3. **Current Value Table** — holds the present logic value of every net.
  4. **Fanout List** — maps each net to the downstream gates it drives.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between compiled simulation and interpreted (event-driven) simulation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Compiled Simulation | Event-Driven Simulation |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | HDL is translated into host C/assembly code; run as a single program. | Simulator interprets the HDL and reacts to signal changes at runtime. |
| Speed | Very fast (one-time compilation). | Slower due to runtime interpretation. |
| Flexibility | Low; recompile for every change. | High; modify and re-run quickly. |
| Best for | Long regression runs. | Early development, debugging. |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of simulation used in VLSI design with their relative trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Introduce the role of simulation in the ASIC flow.

Simulation is the backbone of pre-silicon verification. Since fabrication of a modern IC costs millions of dollars and is irreversible, simulation provides a virtual laboratory.

**Step 2 (4 marks):** Describe each level:
- **Behavioral/RTL Simulation (1.5 marks):** Models algorithmic intent; uses `0, 1, X, Z`; fastest; no timing.
- **Gate-Level Simulation (1.5 marks):** Operates on synthesized netlist with Standard Delay Format (SDF) back-annotation; provides logical and timing accuracy.
- **Switch-Level Simulation (0.5 mark):** Models transistors as switches; bridges logic and circuit.
- **Circuit Simulation (SPICE) (0.5 mark):** Solves Kirchhoff's laws; highest accuracy; slowest.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Trade-off table:
- Speed inversely proportional to accuracy.
- Hierarchy: higher abstraction = faster, less detail.
- KTU-typical choice: gate-level for sign-off; behavioral for early validation.

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the event-driven simulation algorithm. Also derive the condition for the minimum clock period of a sequential circuit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Event-driven algorithm.
1. Initialize all signals; push initial events at time $t = 0$.
2. Pop the earliest event from the time wheel.
3. Update the signal's value.
4. For every fanout gate, re-evaluate the output.
5. If the output changed, schedule a new event at current time + gate delay.
6. Repeat until queue is empty.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Pop Event] --> s2[Update Signal]
    s2 --> s3[Find Fanout]
    s3 --> s4[Evaluate Gate]
    s4 -->|Changed| s5[Schedule Event]
    s4 -->|Same| s1
    s5 --> s1
```

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Derive minimum clock period.
For sequential correctness, the data launched at clock edge $n$ must arrive and stabilize at the destination flip-flop before edge $n+1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su} &\le T_{clk} \\
\therefore T_{clk,\min} &= t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su} \\
f_{clk,\max} &= \frac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** What is a testbench? Explain the components of a self-checking testbench with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Definition.
A **testbench** is an HDL module that wraps the Design Under Test (DUT), generates stimulus, observes responses, and reports pass/fail — without requiring physical pins.

**Step 2 (4 marks):** Components.
1. **Stimulus Generator** — produces clock, reset, and test vectors.
2. **DUT Instantiation** — the design being verified.
3. **Reference Model / Golden Output** — provides expected behavior.
4. **Output Monitor** — captures DUT responses.
5. **Checker** — compares DUT output vs reference.
6. **Reporter** — logs pass/fail and coverage statistics.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Stimulus Gen] --> b1[DUT]
    b1 --> c1[Output Monitor]
    a1 --> c1
    c1 --> d1[Reference Checker]
    ref1[Reference Model] --> d1
    d1 --> e1[Pass Fail Reporter]
```

**(b)** Compare switch-level, gate-level, and circuit-level simulation. Derive the hold time constraint for a sequential design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (4 marks):** Comparison.

| Parameter | Switch-Level | Gate-Level | Circuit (SPICE) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model | Transistor as switch | Boolean gate primitives | Nonlinear differential equations |
| Signal values | 0, 1, X | 0, 1, X, Z | Continuous (V, I) |
| Speed | Medium | Fast | Very slow |
| Accuracy | Approximate | Logical + delay | Physically exact |
| Tool example | IRSIM | ModelSim, VCS | HSPICE, ngspice |

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Hold time derivation.
The minimum (contamination) path delay between two flip-flops must exceed the destination's hold requirement:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{cq,\min} + t_{cd,\min} &\ge t_{hold} \\
\therefore t_{cd,\min} &\ge t_{hold} - t_{cq,\min}
\end{aligned}$$

If the contamination delay is too small, a hold violation occurs and the design fails gate-level simulation timing checks.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose 2–3 marks per answer):**
> 1. **Confusing event-driven with cycle-based simulation.** Cycle-based only evaluates once per clock; event-driven can fire at any time.
> 2. **Writing the propagation delay equation without the setup time term.** KTU strictly requires $T_{clk} \ge t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su}$, not just $t_{cq} + t_{comb}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the fanout effect.** In event-driven simulation, an event is scheduled only for *changed* signals; students often incorrectly say "every gate is evaluated every cycle."
> 4. **Mixing up testbench terminology.** A "monitor" captures; a "checker" compares; a "reporter" logs — these are three separate components in KTU marking.
> 5. **No diagram = 1–2 marks deducted** in 7-mark sub-questions. Always include the event-driven flow block diagram.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Simulation = Pre-fabrication verification** using HDL models and virtual stimuli.
- [x] **Four signal logic values:** $\{0, 1, X, Z\}$.
- [x] **Hierarchy:** Behavioral → Gate → Switch → Circuit (fastest → slowest).
- [x] **Event-driven simulator** uses a **Time Wheel** + **Fanout List** + **Current Value Table**.
- [x] **Compiled simulation** = translate HDL → host code; faster but inflexible.
- [x] **Setup time equation:** $T_{clk} \ge t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su}$.
- [x] **Hold time equation:** $t_{cq,\min} + t_{cd,\min} \ge t_{hold}$.
- [x] **Testbench components:** Stimulus → DUT → Monitor → Checker → Reporter.
- [x] **Coverage metric:** $C = \frac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **KTU favorite question:** Difference between compiled and event-driven simulation.
- [x] **KTU favorite diagram:** Event-driven simulation cycle / time wheel.
- [x] **Tools used in industry:** ModelSim, VCS, QuestaSim, Vivado XSim, Icarus Verilog, ngspice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VHDL Constructs and Codes for Combinational and Sequential Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT415 - DIGITAL SYSTEMS AND VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** VHDL Constructs and Codes for Combinational and Sequential Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is VHDL?

**VHDL (VHSIC Hardware Description Language)** is a formal, strongly-typed, hardware description language used in electronic design automation to describe the structure, behavior, and timing of digital systems. The acronym VHSIC stands for **Very High Speed Integrated Circuit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rigid):** VHDL is a *parallel/concurrent* programming language specifically engineered to model and simulate digital hardware. Unlike sequential software languages (C, Python), every VHDL assignment and process executes in parallel, mirroring the true nature of hardware where millions of transistors operate simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **IEEE Standard:** VHDL was developed by the U.S. Department of Defense in the 1980s and is currently standardized as **IEEE 1076**. It supports four levels of abstraction: **Behavioral, Dataflow, Structural, and Physical/Geometric**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" View

Imagine you are an architect designing a complex building (the digital circuit):

*   The **Entity Declaration** is like the *outer boundary* of the building. It defines the building's name, the entry doors (**inputs/ports `IN`**, e.g., keys, sensors), and the exit doors (**outputs/ports `OUT`**, e.g., lights, elevators). It answers the question: "What does the outside world see?"
*   The **Architecture Body** is the *internal floor plan*. It tells the contractor exactly how the rooms, wires, elevators, and plumbing are connected. It answers: "How does the building actually function inside?"
*   A **Process Block** is a *specialized sub-department* that runs its own internal clock. Within a department, tasks are done strictly one after another (sequentially), but the departments themselves all run simultaneously (concurrently) with respect to each other.
*   A **Signal** is a *physical copper wire* running between departments. A **Variable** is a *scratchpad on a worker's desk*—it exists only inside that worker's office (process).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** VHDL code does *not* execute line-by-line like a C program. Instead, the synthesizer reads your code, understands the intended hardware, and *manufactures* real logic gates. The code is a description, not a procedure.

## 1.3 The Three Modeling Styles in VHDL

| Modeling Style | Level of Abstraction | Intuitive Analogy | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Behavioral** | Highest (Algorithmic) | "Tell me *what* the circuit does" | High-level simulation, state machines, testbenches |
| **Dataflow** | Mid-Level (Register-Transfer) | "Show me the *math/logic equations*" | Combinational circuits (adders, MUX) |
| **Structural** | Lowest (Gate-Level) | "Show me the *wiring diagram* of gates" | Hierarchical design, component instantiation, netlists |

## 1.4 Key KTU Terminology — The "Must-Know" Glossary

*   **Entity:** The interface declaration (black box view).
*   **Architecture:** The internal functional description of an entity.
*   **Port:** A physical input or output pin.
*   **Signal:** A hardware wire (global scope, delayed assignment with `delta` cycles).
*   **Variable:** A local storage inside a process (immediate assignment, software-like).
*   **Process:** A concurrent statement that encloses sequential statements; sensitive to a sensitivity list.
*   **Library / Package:** A collection of pre-compiled, reusable VHDL code (e.g., `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164`).
*   **Configuration:** A binding specification that links an entity to a specific architecture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse `=` (immediate, used for variables) with `<=` (signal assignment, which has a delay). Mixing these is a guaranteed deduction in board exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Concurrent Assignment Styles

### A. Concurrent Signal Assignment (Dataflow)
This statement executes *implicitly in parallel* with all other concurrent statements. It is the workhorse for combinational logic.

```vhdl
Y <= (A AND B) OR (C AND D);  -- This is concurrent, not sequential
```

The synthesizer interprets this as: "Build an AND-OR logic network." There is no "order" of execution; the hardware simply responds to inputs `A, B, C, D`.

### B. Conditional / Selected Concurrent Assignment
Used for clean MUX and decoder descriptions.

*   **`when...else` (Conditional):** Mapped directly to a multiplexer.
*   **`with...select` (Selected):** Mapped to a multiplexer or decoder, requires all cases to be covered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use concurrent over process?** Concurrency makes the code *self-documenting* and unambiguous. It avoids accidental latches (a common KTU question) and clearly shows hardware intent.

## 2.2 The Process Statement — A Sequential Island in a Parallel Ocean

A `process` block is the *only* place where VHDL executes statements strictly in order (top to bottom). It is triggered when **any signal in its sensitivity list changes**.

```vhdl
process(clk, rst)
begin
    if rst = '1' then
        Q <= '0';
    elsif rising_edge(clk) then
        Q <= D;
    end if;
end process;
```

*   **Combinational Process:** Sensitivity list contains *all input signals* (e.g., `process(A, B, Sel)`). No clock, no latches.
*   **Clocked/Sequential Process:** Sensitivity list contains *only `clk` and `rst`*. Uses `rising_edge(clk)`.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Construct Table

| VHDL Construct | Syntax | KTU Purpose | Synthesis Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Entity** | `entity NAME is port(...); end entity;` | Defines external interface | Black-box boundary |
| **Architecture** | `architecture BEH of NAME is ... begin ... end;` | Defines internal behavior | Hardware logic |
| **Concurrent `AND`/`OR`** | `Y <= A and B;` | Dataflow gate modeling | Logic gates |
| **Conditional `when`** | `Y <= A when Sel='1' else B;` | 2:1 MUX | Multiplexer |
| **Selected `with`** | `with Sel select Y <= ...;` | Priority decoder | MUX / Decoder |
| **Process** | `process(sensitivity_list) ... end process;` | Sequential block of code | Combinational or Sequential logic |
| **If-Then-Elsif** | `if A='1' then ... elsif B='1' then ... end if;` | Priority logic, FSMs | Priority encoder / MUX |
| **Case** | `case Sel is when "00" => ... end case;` | One-hot / balanced selection | Decoder / MUX |
| **For Loop** | `for i in 0 to 3 loop ... end loop;` | Bit-sliced hardware (e.g., 4-bit adder) | Unrolled hardware (no software loop) |
| **Signal `<=`** | `Y <= A;` | Hardware wire assignment | Wire with delta delay |
| **Variable `:=`** | `V := A;` | Software-like scratchpad | Wire (synthesized) |
| **Rising_Edge** | `rising_edge(clk)` | Detects 0->1 transition | Edge-triggered flip-flop |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

*   **ASIC Design:** VHDL is the primary language for designing custom chips in smartphones, GPUs, and IoT devices.
*   **FPGA Prototyping:** Engineers use VHDL to program Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (Xilinx, Intel/Altera) for rapid prototyping of hardware before ASIC fabrication.
*   **Verification:** Testbenches written in VHDL are used by companies like Intel, AMD, and Qualcomm to verify billions of transistors before tape-out.
*   **Aerospace & Defense:** High-reliability systems (NASA, Airbus) mandate VHDL for its formal rigor and unambiguous hardware modeling.

## 2.5 The `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164` — The 9-Value Logic System

VHDL uses a 9-state logic system for accurate hardware simulation:

| Value | Meaning | Real Hardware Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `'U'` | Uninitialized | FPGA power-on, RAM contents |
| `'X'` | Forcing Unknown | Contention, short circuit |
| `'0'` | Forcing Low 0 | Strong logic 0 |
| `'1'` | Forcing High 1 | Strong logic 1 |
| `'Z'` | High Impedance | Tri-state buffer output |
| `'W'`, `'L'`, `'H'`, `'-'` | Weak/Don't care | Pull-ups, pull-downs, optimization hints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`std_logic` vs `bit`:** Always use `std_logic` (from `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164`) instead of `bit`. The plain `bit` type only supports `'0'` and `'1'`, missing `'Z'` (essential for bus modeling) and `'X'` (essential for verification).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a `process` with Incomplete `if` Creates a Latch

**Problem Statement:** Why does the following KTU-classic code create a *latch* (a memory element that you did not intend)?

```vhdl
process(A, B, Sel)
begin
    if Sel = '1' then
        Y <= A;
    end if;
end process;
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — The Hardware Translator's Mental Model:**
The VHDL synthesizer reads code and asks: "When `Sel = '0'`, what is the value of `Y`?" The code does not specify, so the synthesizer cannot connect `Y` to a pure combinational wire.

**Step 2 — The Inference of Memory:**
To "remember" the previous value of `Y` (so the output is stable and *defined*) when `Sel = '0'`, the synthesizer must insert a memory element. A memory element that holds its value based on a level-sensitive condition is a **latch**.

**Step 3 — The Formal Conclusion:**
An incomplete `if` (missing `else`) inside a combinational process results in a latch. The latch is a feedback loop: when `Sel = '0'`, the output `Y` holds its last value.

### The Fix (Complete Assignment)
Every code path must assign to `Y`. A complete `if-else` or `case-when others` is mandatory.

```vhdl
process(A, B, Sel)
begin
    if Sel = '1' then
        Y <= A;
    else
        Y <= B;   -- ELSE branch fixes the latch
    end if;
end process;
```

**Result:** This is now a true 2:1 MUX. No latches, pure combinational.

## 3.2 Derivation: Translating Boolean Algebra to Dataflow VHDL

**Given Boolean Equation (KTU standard form):**

$$Y = \overline{A}B + A\overline{B} + \overline{C}$$

**Step 1 — Identify Operators and Variables:**
*   Variables: `A, B, C`
*   Operators: NOT (`\overline{}`), AND (`implicit juxtaposition`), OR (`+`)

**Step 2 — Convert to VHDL Dataflow Syntax:**

$$Y = (not\ A\ and\ B)\ or\ (A\ and\ not\ B)\ or\ (not\ C)$$

```vhdl
Y <= ((not A) and B) or (A and (not B)) or (not C);
```

**Step 3 — Synthesizer Translation:**
The tool builds:
*   Three NOT gates: `not A`, `not B`, `not C`
*   Two 2-input AND gates: `(not A) and B`, `A and (not B)`
*   One 3-input OR gate combining all three terms
*   Net result: 6 transistors per gate, ~24 transistors total.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: `if-elsif` vs `case` — Which to Choose?

| Criterion | `if-elsif-else` | `case-when` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware Mapping** | Priority encoder / MUX | Balanced MUX / Decoder |
| **Speed** | Slower (serial priority check) | Faster (parallel one-hot) |
| **Code Coverage** | `else` optional (creates latch if missing) | `when others` mandatory |
| **Readability for FSMs** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Readability for MUX** | Good | Excellent |
| **Overlap of Conditions** | Allowed (first match wins) | NOT allowed (mutually exclusive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** If the selection is based on the *value of a single signal*, use `case`. If it is based on a *complex boolean expression*, use `if-elsif`.

## 3.4 Derivation: D Flip-Flop from a Clocked Process

**Specification:** A positive-edge-triggered D flip-flop with asynchronous active-low reset.

**Step 1 — The Asynchronous Reset (Highest Priority):**
Asynchronous means the reset acts *immediately* when `rst_n` changes, *without waiting* for the clock edge. This is placed *outside* the `rising_edge` check.

**Step 2 — The Synchronous Data Capture:**
If `rst_n` is not active, then on every rising edge of `clk`, the input `D` is captured into the internal signal `Q`.

**Step 3 — The Complete VHDL Code:**

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity D_FF is
    port ( D      : in  std_logic;
           clk    : in  std_logic;
           rst_n  : in  std_logic;
           Q      : out std_logic);
end D_FF;

architecture BEH of D_FF is
begin
    process(clk, rst_n)
    begin
        if rst_n = '0' then
            Q <= '0';
        elsif rising_edge(clk) then
            Q <= D;
        end if;
    end process;
end BEH;
```

**Synthesis Result:** A master-slave latch pair forming a true edge-triggered flip-flop with 6 internal transistors (master) + 6 (slave) = 12 transistors per flip-flop.

## 4.1 Mermaid: VHDL Code Synthesis & Execution Flow

This diagram maps the journey of a VHDL design from source code to silicon, which is a common KTU question in Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VHDL Source Code .vhd] --> B[Compiler IEEE 1076]

    F --> G[Gate Level Netlist]
    G --> H[Place and Route]

    style J fill:#d4edda
    style E fill:#fff3cd
    style F fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Mermaid: Architecture of a Clocked Process for Sequential Circuits

A visual representation of how a clocked process block is structured — critical for understanding flip-flops and counters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Process]) --> A{Asynchronous Reset Active}
    A -->|Yes| Reset[Drive All Outputs to 0]
    A -->|No| B{Clock Rising Edge}
    B -->|No| Hold[Hold Previous State]
    B -->|Yes| C[Read All Inputs Synchronously]
    C --> D[Execute Sequential Logic]
    D --> E[Update Output Signals]
    E --> F([End Process - Wait for Next Event])
    Reset --> F
    Hold --> F
    
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style B fill:#cce5ff
    style C fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: 4-Bit Synchronous Counter — Structural View

This illustrates how four flip-flops are connected to form a synchronous up-counter, a classic KTU 14-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK[Clock CLK] --> FF0
    RST[Reset RST] --> FF0
    FF0[FF0 Q0] --> FF1[FF1 Q1]
    FF0 --> FF2[FF2 Q2]
    FF0 --> FF3[FF3 Q3]
    FF1 --> FF2
    FF1 --> FF3
    FF2 --> FF3
    
    Q0[Output Q0] --- FF0
    Q1[Output Q1] --- FF1
    Q2[Output Q2] --- FF2
    Q3[Output Q3] --- FF3
    
    style CLK fill:#ffe0b2
    style RST fill:#ffcdd2
    style Q0 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Q1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Q2 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Q3 fill:#c8e6c9
```

**Explanation:** All four flip-flops share the *same clock* (hence "synchronous"). The `enable` logic determines which flip-flops toggle on the next clock edge. This eliminates the cumulative propagation delay of a ripple counter.
