# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Signal and a Variable in VHDL. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Signal | Variable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Assignment Operator** | `<=` (signal assignment) | `:=` (variable assignment) |
| **Update Timing** | Delayed (takes effect at end of `delta` cycle / wait) | Immediate (takes effect instantly) |
| **Scope** | Global, visible across processes | Local, only inside the process |
| **Hardware Equivalence** | Physical wire | Intermediate calculation node |
| **Synthesis** | Generates wires, flip-flops, registers | Typically optimized away into local wires |
| **Example Use** | `Q <= D;` — D Flip-Flop output | `temp := A + B;` — temporary sum inside an adder process |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** "Variables are *visible* only inside their `process`. Signals are the *public wires* that connect the world."

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a `std_logic` type? Why is it preferred over the `bit` type in modern VHDL design?**

**Model Answer:**
`std_logic` is a 9-valued enumerated type defined in the IEEE library `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164`. It supports the values: `'U'`, `'X'`, `'0'`, `'1'`, `'Z'`, `'W'`, `'L'`, `'H'`, `'-'`.

It is preferred over `bit` (which is only 2-valued: `'0'` and `'1'`) because:
1.  It supports `'Z'` (high impedance), essential for modeling tri-state buses.
2.  It supports `'X'` (unknown) and `'U'` (uninitialized) for accurate simulation of real silicon behavior.
3.  It supports `'-'` (don't care), allowing the synthesis tool to optimize the logic.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Design a 4:1 Multiplexer using VHDL. Write the complete VHDL code using (a) Dataflow style and (b) Behavioral style with a process. Explain the difference between concurrent and sequential assignment in your design.**

#### Part (a) — Dataflow Style (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Truth Table Recognition.** A 4:1 MUX selects one of four inputs (`D0, D1, D2, D3`) based on a 2-bit select line `S(1:0)`. The output equation is:

$$Y = \overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0}\,D_0 + \overline{S_1}\,S_0\,D_1 + S_1\,\overline{S_0}\,D_2 + S_1\,S_0\,D_3$$

**Step 2: VHDL Code (Dataflow using `with-select-when`):**

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity MUX4x1_DF is
    port ( D : in  std_logic_vector(3 downto 0);
           S : in  std_logic_vector(1 downto 0);
           Y : out std_logic);
end MUX4x1_DF;

architecture DATAFLOW of MUX4x1_DF is
begin
    with S select
        Y <= D(0) when "00",
             D(1) when "01",
             D(2) when "10",
             D(3) when "11";
end DATAFLOW;
```

**Synthesis Result:** A 4:1 MUX with 2 select bits, ~16 transistors in CMOS implementation.

#### Part (b) — Behavioral Style with Process (7 Marks)

```vhdl
architecture BEHAVIORAL of MUX4x1 is
begin
    process(D, S)
    begin
        case S is
            when "00" => Y <= D(0);
            when "01" => Y <= D(1);
            when "10" => Y <= D(2);
            when "11" => Y <= D(3);
            when others => Y <= 'X';
        end case;
    end process;
end BEHAVIORAL;
```

**Concurrency vs Sequence Explanation:**
*   The `process` is itself a *concurrent* statement (it runs in parallel with other concurrent code in the architecture).
*   *Inside* the process, the `case` statement runs *sequentially* top to bottom.
*   However, since this is combinational logic, the `case` is re-evaluated from scratch every time `D` or `S` changes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common deduction is using `process(clk)` for combinational logic. The sensitivity list MUST include all inputs (`process(D, S)`) for a combinational design. Otherwise, the simulator will miss input changes and the design will fail simulation. **Lose 2 marks** if sensitivity list is wrong.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Design a 4-bit synchronous up-counter with an asynchronous active-low reset. Write the complete VHDL code, draw the synthesized logic block diagram, and explain how the process statement implements a clocked sequential circuit.**

#### Part (a) — VHDL Code (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Design Specification.**
*   4-bit counter: `Q(3 downto 0)` counts 0000 → 1111 → 0000.
*   Clocked: updates on `rising_edge(clk)`.
*   Asynchronous: reset is checked *outside* the clock edge condition.

**Step 2: VHDL Code (Behavioral):**

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;
use IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL;

entity COUNTER_4BIT is
    port ( clk   : in  std_logic;
           rst_n : in  std_logic;
           Q     : out std_logic_vector(3 downto 0));
end COUNTER_4BIT;

architecture BEH of COUNTER_4BIT is
    signal count : std_logic_vector(3 downto 0) := (others => '0');
begin
    process(clk, rst_n)
    begin
        if rst_n = '0' then
            count <= (others => '0');
        elsif rising_edge(clk) then
            count <= count + 1;
        end if;
    end process;
    Q <= count;
end BEH;
```

#### Part (b) — Logic Block Diagram & Explanation (7 Marks)

**Synthesized Block Diagram (4 D Flip-Flops in parallel, sharing a clock and reset):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK[CLK] --> A0[FF0]
    CLK --> A1[FF1]
    CLK --> A2[FF2]
    CLK --> A3[FF3]
    RST[RST_n] --> A0
    RST --> A1
    RST --> A2
    RST --> A3
    A0 --> ADDER[4-bit Adder +1]
    ADDER --> A0
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A0 --> Q0[Q0]
    A1 --> Q1[Q1]
    A2 --> Q2[Q2]
    A3 --> Q3[Q3]
    
    style CLK fill:#ffe0b2
    style RST fill:#ffcdd2
    style ADDER fill:#c8e6c9
```

**Explanation:**
1.  The `process(clk, rst_n)` block is sensitive to the clock and reset.
2.  When `rst_n = '0'`, the asynchronous reset forces `count` to `0000` *immediately*, without waiting for a clock edge.
3.  When `rst_n = '1'` and a rising edge of `clk` occurs, the counter increments by 1.
4.  Between clock edges, the `process` is *suspended* (the synthesizer builds flip-flops to hold the value).
5.  The `<=` operator creates a 4-bit register (4 D flip-flops) plus a 4-bit ripple-carry adder to perform `+1`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Three Critical Errors:**
> 1.  **Forgetting `IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL`:** The `count + 1` arithmetic requires numeric types. Without this library, you get a compile error. **Lose 3 marks.**
> 2.  **Using synchronous reset incorrectly:** A synchronous reset must be *inside* `elsif rising_edge(clk)`. The code above is *asynchronous* because `rst_n` is checked *before* the clock edge. **Lose 2 marks** if confused.
> 3.  **Forgetting to assign `Q <= count`:** The output port is read-only; you must drive it from an internal signal. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam, verify you can answer YES to all of these:**

*   **Entity vs Architecture:** Can define the interface (entity) and behavior (architecture) in under 30 seconds.
*   **Concurrent vs Sequential:** Knows that processes are concurrent *blocks* containing sequential *statements*.
*   **`<=` vs `:=`:** Knows `<=` is for signals (wires, with delay) and `:=` is for variables (immediate, local).
*   **Sensitivity List Rule:** For combinational processes, *all* input signals must be in the sensitivity list. For clocked processes, only `clk` and `rst`.
*   **Latch Inference:** Knows that an incomplete `if` (no `else`) or `case` (no `when others`) inside a process *generates a latch*.
*   **`std_logic` Library:** Always includes `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL` and `IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL` (for arithmetic).
*   **D Flip-Flop Pattern:** `if rst then Q<=0; elsif rising_edge(clk) then Q<=D; end if;` (asynchronous reset).
*   **Counter Pattern:** `elsif rising_edge(clk) then count <= count + 1;`
*   **MUX Patterns:** `with...select` (dataflow) and `case...when` (behavioral) are the two standard ways.
*   **Component Instantiation:** Knows structural VHDL with `port map (formal => actual)`.
*   **9-Value Logic:** Knows `'U'`, `'X'`, `'Z'`, `'-'` and their hardware meanings.
*   **For Loop Unrolling:** Knows that VHDL `for` loops are *unrolled* by the synthesizer — no software loop is generated.
*   **Rising_edge vs Clk'event:** Knows `rising_edge(clk)` is the modern, preferred idiom (it also checks the previous value is `'0'`).
*   **Wait Statement:** Can explain `wait until clk = '1';` inside a process (used in testbenches, not synthesizable for clocked logic).
*   **Configuration Block:** Knows it binds a specific architecture to an entity (less common in basic KTU questions but good to know).
*   **Generate Statement:** Knows `for...generate` for replicating hardware (e.g., an 8-bit adder from 1-bit full adders).

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Mental Model:** "VHDL describes hardware in parallel. Entities are boxes. Architectures are the internals. Processes are sequential islands inside a parallel world. Signals are wires with delay. Variables are scratchpads. `rising_edge(clk)` builds flip-flops. Incomplete `if` builds latches. MUX is `with-select` or `case`. Counter is `count + 1` in a clocked process. *That's the whole module in one breath.*"
